item.title: Re: A minor issue (nitpick?) I had with 4199’s test log
item.content:encoded: I thought the same thing. As soon as the article said there had to be a joe at every site, I immediately thought, "What about 3930? Oh well, I guess 3930 isn't canon with this one." But lo and behold, later in the article, it firmly establishes that 3930 IS canon with this one, fails to address the fact that another person now knows about it, and also reveals its existence to every member of foundation personnel in a completely declassified document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Big upvote from me, but one big(ish?) issue for me too, which I hope can be addressed: The article mentions a few times that the termination1 of a Joe causes a "containment breach". Can you be more specific on this? Does it mean that a random containment breach of any SCP object within foundation custody will randomly breach, or one specific to the site Joe is stationed at? Or is it a Joe specific breach, where some anomalous event occurs when he is terminated?

Footnotes
1. of life or employment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Im not normally interested in factory stuff but this certainly caught my eye, and this new idea for the story behind 053 is really interesting to me.

item.title: A minor issue (nitpick?) I had with 4199’s test log
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3930’s properties and existence is only known to 7 individuals. Exposing SCP-3930’s anomalous property (that is erasing people from existence and then mind screwing others) in SCP-4199 article would be comprimising, since ONLY 7 individuals can know the true nature of 3930 as a pattern screamer.

I like this skip, but I can’t help but wonder how many Foundation employees would know 3930 abilities when they’re intentionally told that 3930 does NOT exist, with the exception of the 7 individuals that were allowed access to the pattern screamer’s file.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the use of crosslinks and the idea of a boring old Joe being conceptually tied to the Foundation got me engaged. But I'm in the same boat as others, the ending was completely anticlimactic. I'm also disappointed because I thought you were going in a completely different direction with all the references and connections in the dream log, but they ultimately lead nowhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's plenty of cool stuff here but the cool stuff is rather disjointed and has a very "forced" seeming connection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, the dream part is cool.
If you intend to edit it, I'd love to expect

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the name supposed to be in the title?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Moose on connecting 087 to 053 but, there were so many crosslinks to Series 1 that, no matter how much narrativic buildup and connections you tried to make, it ultimately fell flat on its face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This felt like it was going somewhere really cool (I thought connecting the staircase and the girl was particularly clever), but then it was just "the Factory" and nothing else really went anywhere. Not enough for me.

Would recommend doing more post-contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i like it a lot! i love the idea of a legion of boring-ass Joes just being present in the Foundation since its inception. i'm imagining 14th-century Josephs askin Foundation precursor members "how 'bout that Plague, huh?" around the drinking pond.

the connection to the Factory seemed a bit unnecessary, though i understand it has to do with the contest theme. it just didn't feel necessary, i was onboard with just an anomalous omnipresent office chode. still like it overall!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: mass produced generic coworker you sort of just tolerate

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a fan of this. Too much cross linking for my tastes - I can't recognise skips by their numbers and I don't want to read every skip linked to understand this one. Plus it's not explained what the containment breach of one of these Joes dying is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh.

This didn't do a whole lot for me, I'm afraid. Joe being the Factory just doesn't mesh with the kind of twisted mass production stuff that we typically see from Factory skips that I find enjoyable. On top of that, the number of crosslinks here reaches a point of being near shoehorning, especially with the test log and containment use. Had it been a smaller, and more finely curated list, I'd be more down, but as is you seem to just be cramming them in there for the sake of cramming them in there. The same could be said about the inclusion of so many of the classic author avatars as well.

I can appreciate the world building attempts here, but the execution just ends up falling flat.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Correct me if I'm wrong, but isn't SCP-2718 a SECRET Scp in the foundation, considering the amount of effort put into hiding it in the foundation database?

So why would another SCP casually link it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You emulated the crosslinking style successfully — I've just never liked that degree of it, but that's a personal preference thing. Overall, this does work as addressing the theme of site history, so I certainly can respect it for hitting its mark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quoting LordMetalton from further downthread:

I liked the buildup here, but the revelation at the end just wasn't enough of a payoff for me.

I infer that ARD is saying1 that the explanation given was not satisfying, and whether or not the Foundation understood the connection, the reader (in this case ARD) did not have enough context to form their own assumptions.

Footnotes
1. I don't want to put words in your mouth, so correct me if I'm wrong

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops! Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There were several dozen crosslinks I thought about using and didn't, as well as several recognizable characters who were left on the cutting room floor. Ultimately, for better or worse, what I wanted was to emulate the crosslinking that was all over Series I and use that to work for the theme of history. Obviously the results were mixed, and it might have contributed to some of the issues I had that I couldn't articulate properly. If I have time, I'll make an edit of this to address some of the concerns in this thread, since many of them were things I wondered about but didn't get conclusive feedback on.1

Footnotes
1. Zyn was neutral about the crosslinking, for example

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the buildup here, but the revelation at the end just wasn't enough of a payoff for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly as much as I hate overly cross referencing things this did it beautifully, at least I think so.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because the foundation doesn't understand the connection either

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you meant writhing here:

My point is that I saw a clear image of that little not-girl in the stairwell inside of this writing mass of black shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My biggest problem with this article is that I never actually felt like the connection between Joe and the Foundation was adequately explained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pushing the limits of how many crosslinks to Series I content I can tolerate. There comes a point at which an article with crosslinks has Too Many and loses its flavor. Like TL333s said about the author avatars, the use of those combined with all the crosslinking is pretty excessive, and I think the testing logs especially detract from and dilute what could have been a really unique standalone concept. I see that some are a fan of that amount of crosslinking, though, so I suppose it comes down to personal writing preferences. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It has become a cliché to withhold information pending clearance level, you flipped this concept on its head while also paying homage to the classics.
Bravo
+1

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: You can edit it now, it's not against the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the general concept, especially the idea that doing anything but letting them be causes a breach, but good lord, did you have to include every single classic author avatar on the site?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gave it some thought and you might be right. I ended up changing it to Keter. It wasn't as separate as I initially thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. It has a good tone all throughout the article, even managing to make Bright sound frightened. I also like how you included various other articles and thought out how they would interact with yours.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The site doesn't need any more special object classes. Heimal was already too much.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
The fruit of my month-long labors, this is my love letter to the site, the mythos, and the bygone days of the Old Guard. Am I 100% satisfied with it? No. It probably will want editing after the contest is over, if it survives. It is, however, acceptable, and I'm happy with how it ended up.

Thanks a million to cabbage for looking the idea over in chat, and to Zyn as usual for their critique. Please let me know if I missed you!
